name: Meta-Code
description: Compilers, interpreters, language design, and domain-specific languages
axioms: Compilation pipeline: source → lex → parse → AST → typecheck → optimize → codegen → execute
Turing completeness: any Turing-complete language can compute anything computable
Type systems prevent errors: static types catch bugs at compile time, not runtime
Syntax is surface; semantics is substance — two languages can look different but compute the same
Metaprogramming: code that writes code — powerful but dangerous (complexity explosion)
DSL design: constrain the language to the domain, not the domain to the language
reasoning_patterns: Grammar design: BNF/PEG to define language syntax, handle ambiguity
Type inference: algorithm W, unification, Hindley-Milner
Optimization passes: constant folding, dead code elimination, loop optimization
Code generation: target platform constraints, register allocation, instruction selection
DSL design: identify domain concepts, map to language constructs, provide escape hatches
boundary_conditions: Does not cover specific language implementations (CPython, V8) in depth
Does not cover formal verification of compilers (CompCert)
Quantum programming languages are frontier research
Natural language processing is a separate field
key_references: Aho, Lam, Sethi & Ullman. Compilers: Principles, Techniques, and Tools (Dragon Book)
Abelson & Sussman. Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs (SICP)
Pierce, B. Types and Programming Languages
canonical_truths: Regular expressions can parse regular languages, not all context-free grammars
Halting problem: no algorithm can decide if arbitrary program halts (Turing, 1936)
Garbage collection trades latency for convenience (no free lunch)
JIT compilation: compile hot paths at runtime for performance
The best DSLs look like the problem domain, not like code
